accepted_findings.source_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), AND (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) EITHER PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY) UP TO A MAXIMUM OF TWO TIMES THE AMOUNT SET FORTH IN THIS SECTION 8.6, (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY) UP TO A MAXIMUM OF TWO TIMES THE AMOUNT SET FORTH IN THIS SECTION 8.6, AND (D) EITHER PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD, IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The unlimited carve-outs for confidentiality breach and IP indemnity create uncapped exposure for both parties. We propose capping these at 2x the base liability cap to ensure predictable maximum exposure while still providing meaningful protection for breaches in these critical areas. We also request adding a standard carve-out for gross negligence, willful misconduct, and fraud.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Unlimited liability for confidentiality breach means any significant data breach or inadvertent disclosure, regardless of your contributory negligence or the absence of actual damages, could create catastrophic exposure. The IP indemnity carve-out similarly creates unlimited exposure for infringement claims even where damages are speculative. In combination, these carve-outs eliminate the practical benefit of having a liability cap.
accepted_findings.position: Cap all carve-outs at 2x the base liability limit. Add standard carve-out for gross negligence, willful misconduct, and fraud only.
accepted_findings.fallback: Cap confidentiality and IP indemnity carve-outs at 2x the base cap. Accept unlimited liability only for gross negligence, willful misconduct, and fraud.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Any structure where confidentiality breach or IP indemnity obligations remain wholly uncapped.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Delaware law does not require unlimited liability for confidentiality or IP claims, and does not preclude capping these obligations by contract.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) EITHER PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY) UP TO A MAXIMUM OF TWO TIMES THE AMOUNT SET FORTH IN THIS SECTION 8.6, (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY) UP TO A MAXIMUM OF TWO TIMES THE AMOUNT SET FORTH IN THIS SECTION 8.6, AND (D) EITHER PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD, IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References standard concerns about unlimited liability and data breach exposure that apply to any SaaS contract, but doesn't engage with this specific deal's structure, the client's low leverage position on their paper, or whether these carve-outs are actually negotiable given the posture.

accepted_findings.source_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.proposed_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay a termination fee equal to fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term, not to exceed six (6) months of Fees
accepted_findings.external_comment: Full tail liability for the remainder of a multi-year term eliminates your ability to exit in response to changed business circumstances, budget constraints, or strategic shifts not constituting provider breach. A liquidated termination fee appropriately compensates the provider for lost revenue while preserving your strategic flexibility.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If you sign a three-year Initial Term and need to exit in year two due to merger, budget reduction, or product pivot, you owe 12 months of full Fees with no corresponding service. In a mid-market SaaS deal this creates six-figure unbudgeted liability and effectively locks you into service you cannot use.
accepted_findings.position: Termination for convenience with 90-day notice and no tail liability beyond the notice period.
accepted_findings.fallback: 50% tail liability capped at six months of Fees, OR ability to assign the agreement to a third party acceptable to the provider.
accepted_findings.walkaway: 100% tail liability for more than six months remaining on term, absent assignment right.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Delaware law enforces liquidated damages if reasonable; full tail liability may be challenged as penalty rather than reasonable forecast of harm.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay a termination fee equal to fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term, not to exceed six (6) months of Fees
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract structure (three-year Initial Term, year-two exit scenario), quantifies the financial exposure (12 months of Fees, six-figure liability), and connects the termination provision to realistic business scenarios (merger, budget reduction, product pivot) under this deal's terms.

accepted_findings.source_text: IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR ANY INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, EXEMPLARY, OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES, OR FOR ANY LOST PROFITS, LOST REVENUE, LOST GOODWILL, LOST OR CORRUPTED DATA, OR COSTS OF SUBSTITUTE GOODS OR SERVICES, ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT, REGARDLESS OF THE FORM OR THEORY OF ACTION AND EVEN IF SUCH PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR ANY INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, EXEMPLARY, OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES, OR FOR ANY LOST PROFITS, LOST REVENUE, LOST GOODWILL, OR COSTS OF SUBSTITUTE GOODS OR SERVICES, ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT, REGARDLESS OF THE FORM OR THEORY OF ACTION AND EVEN IF SUCH PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES. NOTWITHSTANDING THE FOREGOING, THIS WAIVER DOES NOT APPLY TO (A) LOST OR CORRUPTED DATA RESULTING FROM A SECURITY INCIDENT AS DEFINED IN SECTION 5.3, OR (B) CLAIMS ARISING FROM A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY).
accepted_findings.external_comment: The mutual waiver of consequential damages currently includes lost or corrupted data, which may be your primary damages in a data-breach scenario. We request carving out lost-or-corrupted-data claims arising from Security Incidents and confidentiality breaches, so that you can recover your actual losses in those scenarios.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: In a data-breach or confidentiality-breach scenario, your actual harm is often the loss or corruption of Customer Data. Categorizing that as consequential and waiving it means you have no remedy even when the provider breaches its core security and confidentiality obligations. The combination of waiving data loss and carving out confidentiality from the cap creates an internal contradiction: confidentiality breach liability is unlimited in theory but zero in practice because the damages are waived.
accepted_findings.position: Carve out lost or corrupted data from the consequential damages waiver when such loss results from a Security Incident or confidentiality breach.
accepted_findings.fallback: Carve out lost or corrupted Customer Data from the consequential damages waiver only when the loss results from the provider's gross negligence or willful misconduct in connection with a Security Incident.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Delaware courts enforce consequential damages waivers but interpret them against the drafter when they eliminate all remedies for breach of a core obligation.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR ANY INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, EXEMPLARY, OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES, OR FOR ANY LOST PROFITS, LOST REVENUE, LOST GOODWILL, OR COSTS OF SUBSTITUTE GOODS OR SERVICES, ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT, REGARDLESS OF THE FORM OR THEORY OF ACTION AND EVEN IF SUCH PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES. NOTWITHSTANDING THE FOREGOING, THIS WAIVER DOES NOT APPLY TO (A) LOST OR CORRUPTED DATA RESULTING FROM A SECURITY INCIDENT AS DEFINED IN SECTION 5.3, OR (B) CLAIMS ARISING FROM A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY).
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract structure by identifying an internal contradiction between the confidentiality carve-out from the cap and the consequential damages waiver, showing how these provisions interact to leave the client without remedy for data loss.

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Customer if Customer materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail. Customer may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Lattice if Lattice materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if such other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The current provision grants the provider twice the cure period that you receive. For a SaaS agreement where both parties face operational complexity in remedying breaches, mutual cure periods promote balanced risk allocation and reduce the likelihood of inadvertent termination over remediable issues.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Asymmetric cure periods create unequal termination risk. If you miss a payment or violate usage restrictions due to administrative error, you have half the time to cure that the provider has for service failures. In a low-leverage deal posture, 30-day cure exposes you to termination over issues that could be resolved given reasonable time.
accepted_findings.position: Mutual 60-day cure period for material breach.
accepted_findings.fallback: Mutual 45-day cure period, or 30-day cure with explicit carve-out for first-time payment delays.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Delaware common law recognizes good-faith cure efforts; symmetrical cure periods align with that principle.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if such other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific asymmetry in this contract (30 vs 60 days), explains concrete risk scenarios relevant to the client's role (payment/usage violations), and explicitly ties the recommendation to the stated deal posture (low leverage).

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add new Section 4.6: 'Material Breach Defined. For purposes of Section 4.3, a "material breach" means (a) failure to pay undisputed Fees when due, if such failure continues for more than thirty (30) days after written notice; (b) breach of Section 2.2 (Restrictions), Section 5 (Customer Data; Data Security; Privacy), or Section 7 (Confidentiality); (c) any other breach that substantially impairs the other Party's ability to receive the benefit of this Agreement; or (d) repeated breaches of the same or similar obligations, whether or not individually material. Isolated technical performance issues, service interruptions covered by the Service Level Schedule, or good-faith disputes regarding contract interpretation do not constitute material breach.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The termination provision conditions termination on material breach but does not define that term, creating uncertainty as to what triggers the termination right and cure obligation. Defining material breach reduces the risk of unilateral termination based on minor or disputed issues and provides clear notice of which breaches justify termination.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without a definition, the provider could assert that any service complaint, usage-limit exceedance, or payment dispute is material and trigger a 30-day cure-or-terminate scenario. You face termination risk over issues both parties might consider minor. In a subscription relationship spanning multiple years, definitional clarity prevents strategic termination threats over non-substantive issues.
accepted_findings.position: Insert materiality definition scoping termination to breaches that substantially impair contract benefit, with specific carve-outs for service-level issues and good-faith disputes.
accepted_findings.fallback: Definition limited to payment default, confidentiality breach, or breach causing direct financial harm exceeding a specified threshold.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Delaware courts interpret material breach contextually; parties may define it by contract, and doing so reduces litigation risk.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add new Section 4.6: 'Material Breach Defined. For purposes of Section 4.3, a "material breach" means (a) failure to pay undisputed Fees when due, if such failure continues for more than thirty (30) days after written notice; (b) breach of Section 2.2 (Restrictions), Section 5 (Customer Data; Data Security; Privacy), or Section 7 (Confidentiality); (c) any other breach that substantially impairs the other Party's ability to receive the benefit of this Agreement; or (d) repeated breaches of the same or similar obligations, whether or not individually material. Isolated technical performance issues, service interruptions covered by the Service Level Schedule, or good-faith disputes regarding contract interpretation do not constitute material breach.'
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Rationale engages with the specific contract structure (30-day cure-or-terminate mechanism, multi-year subscription relationship) and identifies concrete risk scenarios (service complaints, usage limits, payment disputes) that flow from the absence of a materiality definition in this particular agreement.

accepted_findings.source_text: The arbitration will be conducted in San Francisco, California by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: The arbitration will be conducted in the city where Customer's principal place of business is located, or in San Francisco, California if Customer's principal place of business is outside the continental United States, by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Exclusive arbitration in San Francisco imposes travel, counsel, and logistical costs on any buyer not headquartered in the Bay Area. A floating venue tied to your principal place of business balances access to dispute resolution without materially increasing the provider's burden given that arbitration is designed to accommodate remote participation.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If you are headquartered on the East Coast or internationally, mandatory San Francisco arbitration adds significant travel and local-counsel costs to any mid-size dispute. For claims under the liability cap, the forum cost may exceed the recovery, effectively eliminating your remedy.
accepted_findings.position: Floating venue: arbitration in the city where your principal place of business is located.
accepted_findings.fallback: Venue in your city for claims you initiate; San Francisco for claims the provider initiates.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware governing law, but arbitration situs affects logistical cost and witness availability regardless of substantive law.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: The arbitration will be conducted in the city where Customer's principal place of business is located, or in San Francisco, California if Customer's principal place of business is outside the continental United States, by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract's San Francisco venue requirement and connects it to the client's potential geographic position and the contract's liability cap structure, showing how forum costs interact with recovery limits to create practical remedy concerns.

accepted_findings.source_text: errors and omissions / professional liability insurance (which may include cyber liability coverage) with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate
accepted_findings.proposed_text: errors and omissions / professional liability insurance with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate; and (c) cyber liability insurance (which may be included within the errors and omissions policy or maintained as a separate policy) with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate, covering unauthorized access to or acquisition of Customer Data, data breaches, and privacy regulatory defense and penalties
accepted_findings.external_comment: The Subscription Services process Customer Data in a cloud-hosted environment, creating direct cyber risk. The current language makes cyber coverage optional. We propose requiring dedicated cyber liability coverage with explicit scope covering data breaches and privacy regulatory exposure.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The provider hosts and processes Customer Data. A data breach affecting Customer Data could trigger regulatory penalties, notification costs, and third-party claims. Without required cyber coverage, you bear the risk that the provider's errors and omissions policy excludes cyber losses, leaving you to pursue damages against the provider's general assets subject to the 12-month-fees liability cap.
accepted_findings.position: Require dedicated cyber liability insurance at $5M per occurrence and aggregate, with scope covering unauthorized access to Customer Data, data breaches, privacy regulatory defense, and penalties.
accepted_findings.fallback: Require that the errors and omissions policy expressly include cyber liability coverage at the $5M limit, with written confirmation in the certificate of insurance that cyber losses are covered.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: United States (Delaware substantive law, but cyber-breach notification obligations vary by state; assuming multi-state exposure given cloud-hosted service).
accepted_findings.suggested_text: errors and omissions / professional liability insurance with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate; and (c) cyber liability insurance (which may be included within the errors and omissions policy or maintained as a separate policy) with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate, covering unauthorized access to or acquisition of Customer Data, data breaches, and privacy regulatory defense and penalties
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: The rationale engages with this specific contract by referencing the 12-month-fees liability cap and the interplay between the E&O policy exclusions and recovery limitations, showing how the insurance gap creates risk under this deal's specific structure.

accepted_findings.source_text: Neither Party will be liable for any delay or failure to perform its obligations under this Agreement (other than payment obligations) to the extent such delay or failure is caused by an event beyond such Party's reasonable control
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Neither Party will be liable for any delay or failure to perform its non-monetary obligations under this Agreement to the extent such delay or failure is caused by an event beyond such Party's reasonable control and such Party could not have prevented the delay or failure through the exercise of reasonable care. Customer's obligation to pay Fees is suspended during any period in which Lattice is unable to provide the Subscription Services due to a Force Majeure Event affecting Lattice.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The current carve-out requires you to pay Fees during extended force majeure events even when the provider cannot deliver service. If the provider's infrastructure suffers a two-month outage due to a force majeure event, you receive no service but owe full payment. Suspending the Fee obligation during provider-side force majeure aligns payment with service delivery and is standard in SaaS agreements.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If a force majeure event prevents the provider from providing service for 60 days, the force majeure clause gives either party the right to terminate, but does not suspend payment obligations during the event. You pay two months of Fees for zero service, and if you terminate, the already-accrued Fees are unrecoverable.
accepted_findings.position: Suspend your Fee obligation during any period the provider cannot provide Subscription Services due to force majeure. Extend Subscription Term by the suspension period.
accepted_findings.fallback: Pro-rata credit for any full calendar week during which service is unavailable due to provider-side force majeure, up to 100% of monthly Fee.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Delaware law enforces force majeure clauses as written; absence of payment suspension exposes you to pay-without-service scenario.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Neither Party will be liable for any delay or failure to perform its non-monetary obligations under this Agreement to the extent such delay or failure is caused by an event beyond such Party's reasonable control and such Party could not have prevented the delay or failure through the exercise of reasonable care. Customer's obligation to pay Fees is suspended during any period in which Lattice is unable to provide the Subscription Services due to a Force Majeure Event affecting Lattice.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract's payment structure during force majeure by identifying a concrete scenario (60-day event triggering termination rights) and tracing through the financial consequence under this contract's clauses, though stops short of weighing deal posture or trading off against other terms.

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by up to five percent (5%) annually, or the percentage increase in the United States Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers (CPI-U) for the twelve-month period ending on the last day of the then-current Subscription Term, whichever is greater, by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any increase in excess of this cap requires Customer's written consent. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We propose capping annual fee increases at the greater of 5% or CPI-U, with any larger increase requiring mutual agreement. This provides budget predictability for multi-year planning while allowing the provider to adjust for genuine cost inflation.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Uncapped renewal increases combined with automatic renewal and non-refundable fees create unbounded budget risk. The provider could unilaterally impose a 20% or 30% increase at each renewal, forcing you either to accept or to migrate platforms mid-term at substantial switching cost. A 5%/CPI cap preserves the provider's inflation adjustment while making your long-term costs plannable.
accepted_findings.position: Cap at greater of 5% or CPI-U annually; increases beyond the cap require your written consent.
accepted_findings.fallback: Cap at greater of 7% or CPI-U; or allow uncapped increases but grant you a 90-day termination right if the proposed increase exceeds 10%.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Delaware contract law enforces renewal-increase provisions as written; no statutory cap exists for commercial software agreements.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by up to five percent (5%) annually, or the percentage increase in the United States Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers (CPI-U) for the twelve-month period ending on the last day of the then-current Subscription Term, whichever is greater, by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any increase in excess of this cap requires Customer's written consent. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract structure by connecting three provisions (uncapped increases, automatic renewal, non-refundable fees) to show how they compound risk, and proposes a cap that balances both parties' interests given this deal's architecture.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 3.2A Disputed Amounts. Customer may withhold payment of any amount that is subject to a good-faith dispute, provided that Customer (a) pays all undisputed amounts by the due date, (b) provides Lattice with written notice of the disputed amount and the basis for such dispute within fifteen (15) days of receipt of the applicable invoice, and (c) works in good faith with Lattice to resolve the dispute within thirty (30) days of such notice. If the Parties are unable to resolve the dispute within such thirty-day period, either Party may pursue its remedies under Section 10.2.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We propose adding a provision permitting you to withhold disputed invoice amounts while preserving payment obligations on undisputed portions. This protects your ability to challenge billing errors or overcharges without triggering late fees or suspension on the full invoice amount.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without dispute-withholding rights, you are forced to pay potentially incorrect or excessive charges in full to avoid late fees and service suspension, then attempt recovery post-payment. In a contract with annual advance billing and uncapped renewal increases, this creates a material cash-flow disadvantage and eliminates your leverage to negotiate billing disputes in good faith.
accepted_findings.position: Insert a new section granting you the right to withhold only the specific disputed amount, with notice within 15 days of invoice receipt and good-faith negotiation for 30 days before arbitration.
accepted_findings.fallback: Withholding right with shortened notice and negotiation period; requires payment into escrow if dispute exceeds 30 days.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Delaware common law supports good-faith dispute resolution but does not mandate contractual withholding rights in commercial agreements.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 3.2A Disputed Amounts. Customer may withhold payment of any amount that is subject to a good-faith dispute, provided that Customer (a) pays all undisputed amounts by the due date, (b) provides Lattice with written notice of the disputed amount and the basis for such dispute within fifteen (15) days of receipt of the applicable invoice, and (c) works in good faith with Lattice to resolve the dispute within thirty (30) days of such notice. If the Parties are unable to resolve the dispute within such thirty-day period, either Party may pursue its remedies under Section 10.2.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: The rationale engages with specific contract structure (annual advance billing, uncapped renewal increases) and explains how the absence of withholding rights creates leverage problems in this particular deal context, rather than just stating general principles about dispute resolution.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 6.4 Professional Services Deliverables. Notwithstanding Section 2.5, any deliverables created specifically for Customer pursuant to a Statement of Work, including custom configurations, reports, analyses, and implementation documentation ("Custom Deliverables"), shall be the sole property of Customer upon full payment of the applicable Fees. Lattice retains all right, title, and interest in its pre-existing materials, methodologies, tools, and know-how, and Customer receives a non-exclusive, perpetual, royalty-free license to use any such pre-existing materials incorporated in Custom Deliverables solely in connection with Customer's use of the Subscription Services or any successor system.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The contract states that the provider retains all rights in methodology, tools, and pre-existing materials used in Professional Services, but does not address ownership of deliverables created specifically for you under a Statement of Work. Industry standard for professional services is that custom deliverables belong to the customer who paid for them, with the service provider retaining rights only to its pre-existing IP. We propose clarifying that you own custom work product while the provider retains its background IP.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If Custom Deliverables created specifically for you under a Statement of Work belong to the provider, you cannot use them after termination or with a successor vendor, and you have paid for work product you do not own. For Professional Services engagements costing tens to hundreds of thousands of dollars, this creates significant economic exposure and vendor lock-in.
accepted_findings.position: You own all deliverables created specifically for you pursuant to a Statement of Work. The provider retains its pre-existing materials, methodologies, and tools, and receives a non-exclusive license to use general-applicability improvements derived from the engagement.
accepted_findings.fallback: You own Custom Deliverables upon payment; the provider retains pre-existing IP and may reuse methodologies, tools, and learnings in anonymized form for other customers.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Provider ownership of custom work product paid for by you without perpetual license back to you for such work product.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Under Delaware law, absent clear contractual assignment, work-for-hire analysis would apply to professional services deliverables.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 6.4 Professional Services Deliverables. Notwithstanding Section 2.5, any deliverables created specifically for Customer pursuant to a Statement of Work, including custom configurations, reports, analyses, and implementation documentation ("Custom Deliverables"), shall be the sole property of Customer upon full payment of the applicable Fees. Lattice retains all right, title, and interest in its pre-existing materials, methodologies, tools, and know-how, and Customer receives a non-exclusive, perpetual, royalty-free license to use any such pre-existing materials incorporated in Custom Deliverables solely in connection with Customer's use of the Subscription Services or any successor system.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References general principles about work product ownership and vendor lock-in in professional services engagements, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's structure, the client's deal posture (low leverage on their paper), or whether pushing on this point is feasible or advisable given the context.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 8.7: IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR ANY INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, EXEMPLARY, OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES, OR FOR ANY LOST PROFITS, LOST REVENUE, LOST GOODWILL, LOST OR CORRUPTED DATA, OR COSTS OF SUBSTITUTE GOODS OR SERVICES
accepted_findings.proposed_text: ANNOTATE INTERNAL CONTRADICTION: The accepted edit to Section 8.7 (risk-allocation-analyst-003) removes 'LOST OR CORRUPTED DATA' from the consequential damages waiver for Security Incidents and confidentiality breaches. However, Section 8.6 carves out confidentiality breaches from the liability cap entirely, creating UNLIMITED liability. The combination means: (1) Confidentiality breach liability is unlimited per Section 8.6(B); (2) Lost-or-corrupted-data damages are now recoverable per the edited Section 8.7; (3) Therefore lost-or-corrupted-data damages from confidentiality breach are UNLIMITED AND UNCAPPED. This creates catastrophic exposure that contradicts the intent of finding risk-allocation-analyst-001, which sought to CAP confidentiality liability at 2x the base cap. The accepted edits are internally inconsistent. RECOMMENDATION: Either (a) accept risk-allocation-analyst-001's proposed cap on confidentiality breach at 2x, making the Section 8.7 carve-out workable, OR (b) reject the Section 8.7 carve-out for confidentiality breach and limit it only to Security Incidents, OR (c) add express language to Section 8.7 that the carve-out is subject to the Section 8.6 cap structure.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Critical internal contradiction between accepted findings: The edit to Section 8.7 allowing recovery of lost-or-corrupted-data damages for confidentiality breaches combines with Section 8.6's unlimited confidentiality carve-out to create unlimited data-loss liability. This contradicts the parallel effort in risk-allocation-analyst-001 to cap confidentiality exposure. The redline as currently accepted would make confidentiality breach liability unlimited for the most common category of actual damages (data loss), which is almost certainly not your intent and creates unquantifiable exposure.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: As currently drafted with both accepted edits, any confidentiality breach resulting in lost or corrupted Customer Data triggers unlimited liability with no cap. A single inadvertent disclosure event affecting your data could result in damages orders of magnitude beyond the 12-month-fees base cap. This is the highest-severity internal contradiction in the accepted findings because it converts a negotiated liability framework into an unlimited-exposure scenario for the most probable harm (data breach). The signing attorney and business owner will face unlimited exposure they believe was capped.
accepted_findings.position: Resolve the contradiction by either accepting the 2x cap on confidentiality in risk-allocation-analyst-001 (making the Section 8.7 carve-out bounded), or limiting the Section 8.7 carve-out to Security Incidents only and excluding confidentiality breach.
accepted_findings.fallback: Add express language to the edited Section 8.7 stating: 'For the avoidance of doubt, any damages recoverable under this carve-out remain subject to the liability cap and carve-out structure set forth in Section 8.6, as amended.'
accepted_findings.suggested_text: ANNOTATE INTERNAL CONTRADICTION: The accepted edit to Section 8.7 (risk-allocation-analyst-003) removes 'LOST OR CORRUPTED DATA' from the consequential damages waiver for Security Incidents and confidentiality breaches. However, Section 8.6 carves out confidentiality breaches from the liability cap entirely, creating UNLIMITED liability. The combination means: (1) Confidentiality breach liability is unlimited per Section 8.6(B); (2) Lost-or-corrupted-data damages are now recoverable per the edited Section 8.7; (3) Therefore lost-or-corrupted-data damages from confidentiality breach are UNLIMITED AND UNCAPPED. This creates catastrophic exposure that contradicts the intent of finding risk-allocation-analyst-001, which sought to CAP confidentiality liability at 2x the base cap. The accepted edits are internally inconsistent. RECOMMENDATION: Either (a) accept risk-allocation-analyst-001's proposed cap on confidentiality breach at 2x, making the Section 8.7 carve-out workable, OR (b) reject the Section 8.7 carve-out for confidentiality breach and limit it only to Security Incidents, OR (c) add express language to Section 8.7 that the carve-out is subject to the Section 8.6 cap structure.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract structure by identifying the interaction between two accepted provisions (Section 8.7 carve-out and the 2x cap), explains the concrete consequence under this contract (unlimited liability for data breach despite base 12-month-fees cap), and references the specific harm scenario created by the contradiction.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b): if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.proposed_text: ANNOTATE INTERNAL INCONSISTENCY: The accepted edit to Section 4.4(b) (termination-remedies-analyst-002) replaces 100% tail liability with '50% of Fees... not to exceed six (6) months of Fees' for Customer termination without cause. However, the newly inserted Section 4.6 (termination-remedies-analyst-003) defines 'material breach' to include payment default continuing more than 30 days after notice. Under the edited framework: (1) If you miss payment for 30+ days, that is a defined material breach under Section 4.6(a); (2) Provider terminates under Section 4.3 with 30-day cure; (3) You then owe the REDUCED tail liability (50%, max 6 months) under the edited Section 4.4(b) because the termination is ostensibly 'by Customer' even though it was triggered by Provider notice of breach. The definition of material breach in Section 4.6 makes payment default a termination trigger, but the tail-liability edit in Section 4.4(b) does not distinguish between Customer voluntary termination and Provider termination for Customer breach. This creates ambiguity as to whether breach-termination by Provider triggers the old 100% tail or the new 50%/6-month cap. RECOMMENDATION: Clarify that the reduced tail liability in Section 4.4(b) applies only to voluntary Customer termination for convenience (which does not exist under the Agreement as written), and that termination by Provider for Customer breach results in 100% tail liability.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The tail-liability edit and the material-breach definition interact to create ambiguity. If the provider terminates for your payment default (a defined material breach under the new Section 4.6), it is unclear whether you owe 100% of remaining Fees or the reduced 50%/6-month amount. The edited Section 4.4(b) references 'if Customer terminates... other than for Lattice's uncured material breach,' but does not address the scenario where Lattice terminates for Customer's breach. This gap could be exploited in a dispute.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The ambiguity creates a five- or six-figure tail-liability dispute in any scenario where the provider terminates for your breach. If the provider terminates in month 6 of a 36-month term due to your payment default, do you owe 30 months of full Fees (100% tail) or 6 months of half Fees (50% cap)? The accepted edits do not resolve this, and the provider will argue for the higher amount. The language should be tightened to avoid litigation over this threshold issue.
accepted_findings.position: Clarify that the reduced tail liability applies only when you terminate voluntarily, and that provider termination for your material breach results in 100% tail liability for the remaining term.
accepted_findings.fallback: Clarify that the reduced tail applies to any termination by either party other than provider termination for your uncured material breach of payment, confidentiality, or usage restrictions.

accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with specific contract mechanics (provider termination in month 6 of 36-month term, payment default scenario), quantifies the dollar exposure (five- or six-figure dispute), and identifies the specific gap the accepted edits fail to resolve, grounded in the termination clause structure.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 9.3: Customer's obligation to pay Fees is suspended during any period in which Lattice is unable to provide the Subscription Services due to a Force Majeure Event affecting Lattice.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: ANNOTATE POTENTIAL CONFLICT WITH SECTION 2.3 SLA SOLE REMEDY: The accepted edit to Section 9.3 (termination-remedies-analyst-006) suspends your Fee obligation when the provider cannot deliver service due to force majeure. However, Section 2.3 states that service credits are your 'sole and exclusive remedies for failures to meet such service-level commitments' and that 'Customer's sole remedy in the event of repeated failures shall be the right to terminate.' If a force majeure event causes a service outage, it is unclear whether: (1) the outage is excluded from SLA calculations (making Section 2.3 inapplicable and the force majeure fee suspension the operative remedy), or (2) the outage counts against SLA and triggers service credits under Section 2.3, in which case the force majeure fee suspension in Section 9.3 contradicts the sole-remedy framing in Section 2.3. The contract does not state that force majeure events are excluded from SLA measurement. RECOMMENDATION: Add express language to Section 2.3 or Section 9.3 stating that force majeure events are excluded from SLA availability calculations, and that the fee-suspension mechanism in Section 9.3 is the sole remedy for force-majeure-caused outages.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The force majeure fee suspension you negotiated may conflict with the SLA sole-remedy provision. If a two-week provider-side force majeure outage triggers both the SLA service-credit mechanism (Section 2.3) and the force majeure fee suspension (edited Section 9.3), the provider may argue that service credits are your sole remedy and fee suspension does not apply. Alternatively, you may argue fee suspension applies but service credits do not. The contract should clarify which regime governs force-majeure-caused outages.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: In a major force majeure event causing extended outage (e.g., AWS region failure, pandemic-related infrastructure collapse), the difference between service credits (typically 10–25% of monthly fee as a credit) and full fee suspension (100% of fee for the outage period) is significant. The ambiguity creates a five-figure dispute in any such event. Clarifying that force majeure outages are excluded from SLA and governed solely by the fee-suspension mechanism eliminates the conflict and ensures you receive the negotiated relief.
accepted_findings.position: Add language to Section 9.3 or Section 2.3 clarifying that force majeure events are excluded from SLA calculations and that the fee-suspension mechanism is the sole remedy for force-majeure-caused service interruptions.
accepted_findings.fallback: Clarify that force majeure fee suspension applies in addition to (not instead of) SLA service credits, and that you receive both remedies if a force majeure event also breaches SLA commitments.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: ANNOTATE POTENTIAL CONFLICT WITH SECTION 2.3 SLA SOLE REMEDY: The accepted edit to Section 9.3 (termination-remedies-analyst-006) suspends your Fee obligation when the provider cannot deliver service due to force majeure. The contract does not state that force majeure events are excluded from SLA measurement. RECOMMENDATION: Add express language to Section 2.3 or Section 9.3 stating that force majeure events are excluded from SLA availability calculations, and that the fee-suspension mechanism in Section 9.3 is the sole remedy for force-majeure-caused outages.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract's dual-mechanism structure (SLA credits vs. fee suspension), quantifies the financial exposure (five-figure dispute), and reasons through how the ambiguity creates risk under this particular contract's provisions.

rejected_findings.proposed_text: 8.3A Customer IP Indemnity. Customer will defend Lattice against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that Customer Data, or Customer's use of the Subscription Services in breach of this Agreement or applicable law, infringes or violates such third party's intellectual property rights or other legal rights (a "Customer Infringement Claim"), and will indemnify Lattice for any damages finally awarded against Lattice (or amounts paid in settlement approved by Customer) in connection with any Customer Infringement Claim. Customer's obligations under this Section 8.3A are subject to the indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5 and are subject to the liability cap and carve-outs set forth in Section 8.6.
rejected_findings.external_comment: The Agreement contains a comprehensive IP indemnity running from the provider to you, but no reciprocal indemnity from you to the provider for claims arising from Customer Data or your misuse of the Subscription Services. This creates an asymmetric allocation of risk where the provider bears all third-party IP risk even when you are the source of the allegedly infringing content. We request a standard reciprocal indemnity.
rejected_findings.materiality_rationale: You upload data to the platform. If that data contains third-party copyrighted content, trade secrets, or other protected material, the provider faces uncapped third-party liability with no indemnity from you. In a typical SaaS relationship, the data provider indemnifies the platform for claims arising from the data.
rejected_findings.position: Mutual IP indemnity: you indemnify the provider for claims arising from Customer Data and your misuse of the Services, subject to the same cap structure as the provider's IP indemnity.
rejected_findings.fallback: Your IP indemnity limited to third-party claims arising from Customer Data only, and subject to a 2x cap rather than unlimited.
rejected_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Delaware law recognizes mutual indemnity obligations and does not require asymmetric allocation in commercial SaaS agreements.

rejected_findings.rejection_reason: LLM escalation: edit moves contract against client in role.

auditor_findings.source_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), AND (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY.
auditor_findings.proposed_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) EITHER PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), UP TO A MAXIMUM OF THE GREATER OF (i) TWO TIMES THE AMOUNT SET FORTH IN THIS SECTION 8.6 OR (ii) FIVE MILLION U.S. DOLLARS ($5,000,000), (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), UP TO A MAXIMUM OF THE GREATER OF (i) TWO TIMES THE AMOUNT SET FORTH IN THIS SECTION 8.6 OR (ii) FIVE MILLION U.S. DOLLARS ($5,000,000), (D) EITHER PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD, AND (E) LOST OR CORRUPTED CUSTOMER DATA RESULTING FROM A SECURITY INCIDENT AS DEFINED IN SECTION 5.3, UP TO THE GREATER OF (i) TWO TIMES THE AMOUNT SET FORTH IN THIS SECTION 8.6 OR (ii) FIVE MILLION U.S. DOLLARS ($5,000,000), IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY.
auditor_findings.external_comment: The combination of unlimited confidentiality-breach liability (§8.6(b)), mutual waiver of lost-or-corrupted-data damages (§8.7), Security Incident notification obligations (§5.3), and the stated $5M E&O/cyber insurance requirement (§9.2) creates an existential cross-section hazard that no individual specialist caught: In a major data breach, Customer's primary damages (lost or corrupted Customer Data) are categorically waived under §8.7, yet confidentiality breach triggers unlimited liability under §8.6(b). This means: (1) Customer cannot recover its actual losses even though Lattice breached core security obligations; (2) the unlimited carve-out exposes both parties to bankruptcy risk disproportionate to the deal economics; and (3) the $5M insurance requirement signals parties' actual risk tolerance but the contract creates exposure orders of magnitude higher. Risk-allocation-analyst flagged pieces of this but undershot the existential character of the combined effect.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Cross-section analysis: Risk-allocation-analyst correctly flagged unlimited confidentiality/IP carve-outs (finding 001) and the lost-data waiver (finding 003) as separate issues. But the specialists' domain-siloed review missed that these provisions operate simultaneously in a data-breach scenario to create a lose-lose outcome: Customer pays unlimited damages for confidentiality breach but cannot itself recover for the actual loss (Customer Data). If Customer's annual fees are $500K, the base cap is $500K, but a data breach affecting 100K records could trigger $10M+ in regulatory penalties, notification costs, and third-party claims—all on Customer under the unlimited carve-out, with zero recovery for Customer's own losses under the consequential-damages waiver. For a low-leverage buyer with unknown industry (potentially regulated: healthcare, financial services, government contractor), this combination is existential because a single breach event could bankrupt Customer while leaving Customer without remedy. The insurance requirement at $5M confirms that $5M is the parties' actual expectation of maximum exposure, yet the contract creates unlimited upside risk. No specialist escalated this to existential because each looked only at their piece; only cross-section review reveals that the contract is internally incoherent and creates mutual destruction risk.
auditor_findings.position: Cap all carve-outs at the greater of 2x base cap or $5M (matching insurance). Add explicit carve-out from consequential-damages waiver for lost or corrupted Customer Data resulting from Security Incidents or confidentiality breach, subject to same super-cap. Add carve-out for gross negligence, willful misconduct, and fraud (standard).
auditor_findings.fallback: Cap confidentiality and Security Incident exposure at $5M (insurance limit) only. Accept unlimited IP indemnity but negotiate insurance increase to $10M to cover it. Retain consequential-damages waiver for data loss but require Lattice to maintain dedicated cyber insurance with proof that data-loss claims are covered.
auditor_findings.walkaway: Any structure where data-breach liability remains unlimited while actual data-loss damages are waived, creating uninsurable and unbounded exposure for Customer with no corresponding remedy.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (§10.1). Delaware courts enforce liability caps and carve-outs as written but will not enforce internally contradictory provisions that eliminate all remedies for breach of core obligations. The combination here may fail coherence analysis under Delaware contract-construction principles.
auditor_findings.suggested_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) EITHER PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), UP TO A MAXIMUM OF THE GREATER OF (i) TWO TIMES THE AMOUNT SET FORTH IN THIS SECTION 8.6 OR (ii) FIVE MILLION U.S. DOLLARS ($5,000,000), (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), UP TO A MAXIMUM OF THE GREATER OF (i) TWO TIMES THE AMOUNT SET FORTH IN THIS SECTION 8.6 OR (ii) FIVE MILLION U.S. DOLLARS ($5,000,000), (D) EITHER PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD, AND (E) LOST OR CORRUPTED CUSTOMER DATA RESULTING FROM A SECURITY INCIDENT AS DEFINED IN SECTION 5.3, UP TO THE GREATER OF (i) TWO TIMES THE AMOUNT SET FORTH IN THIS SECTION 8.6 OR (ii) FIVE MILLION U.S. DOLLARS ($5,000,000), IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY.

auditor_findings.source_text: Upon any expiration or termination of this Agreement: (a) Customer's right to access and use the Subscription Services will immediately terminate; (b) Customer will pay Lattice all Fees due and payable as of the effective date of termination, and, if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term;
auditor_findings.proposed_text: Upon any expiration or termination of this Agreement: (a) Customer's right to access and use the Subscription Services will immediately terminate; (b) Customer will pay Lattice all Fees due and payable as of the effective date of termination; (c) if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, and only if such termination occurs after the first twelve (12) months of the Initial Term, Customer shall pay Lattice a termination fee equal to fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term, not to exceed six (6) months of Fees, and Lattice shall refund to Customer any prepaid Fees for the period after the effective date of termination, less such termination fee;
auditor_findings.external_comment: The combination of annual advance billing (§3.2), 100% non-refundable tail liability for remainder of term (§4.4(b)), asymmetric 30-day cure period for Customer breach (§4.3), and no termination-for-convenience right creates a cross-section trap that effectively locks Customer into a multi-year financial commitment with no economic exit and asymmetric termination risk. Commercial-terms-analyst and termination-remedies-analyst each flagged pieces of this, but the combination is existential because it eliminates Customer's ability to respond to changed circumstances (merger, budget cuts, product pivot, vendor performance issues not rising to material breach) without forfeiting six to seven figures of prepaid fees while receiving zero service.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Cross-section analysis: Commercial-terms-analyst flagged 100% tail liability as 'moderate' (finding 006), termination-remedies-analyst flagged it as 'blocker' (finding 002), but neither escalated to existential. The existential character emerges only when you combine: (1) annual advance billing means Customer has already paid 12 months of fees; (2) no termination-for-convenience means Customer cannot exit mid-term absent Lattice material breach; (3) 30-day cure period for Customer (vs. 60 days for Lattice) means Customer faces faster termination exposure; (4) if Customer attempts to exit in month 6 of a 36-month Initial Term having prepaid $500K, Customer owes the remaining 30 months ($1.25M) AND forfeits the 6 months of prepaid service ($250K) for total exposure of $1.5M with zero service going forward. In a low-leverage their-paper scenario with no profile data, this is a walk-away issue because it creates vendor lock-in and financial exposure that no prudent buyer would accept absent extreme discount or strategic necessity. For a company facing acquisition, budget reallocation, or platform consolidation, this structure makes the contract an immovable liability on the balance sheet. The 100% tail + prepaid structure + no TFC operates as a penalty, not liquidated damages (Lattice's marginal cost of continued SaaS delivery is near zero; forfeiting $1.5M for $0 service is punitive). Delaware law disfavors penalties disguised as liquidated damages, and this may be unenforceable, but litigation risk is itself existential for Customer.
auditor_findings.position: Customer termination for convenience with 90 days' notice and no tail liability beyond the notice period, OR 50% tail liability capped at six months of Fees with refund of prepaid amounts less the termination fee, and mutual 60-day cure periods.
auditor_findings.fallback: 50% tail liability capped at six months, refund of prepaid fees less termination fee, but termination for convenience available only after first 12 months of Initial Term. Retain 30-day Customer cure but add 10-day grace period for first-time payment default before breach notice.
auditor_findings.walkaway: 100% tail liability for more than six months remaining on term, combined with annual advance billing and no assignment right. This structure creates $1M+ unbudgeted exposure in any multi-year deal and eliminates Customer's strategic flexibility.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (§10.1). Delaware courts analyze liquidated-damages provisions for reasonableness; 100% forfeiture of prepaid SaaS fees with no service and no marginal cost to provider likely constitutes unenforceable penalty. But litigation risk is material.

If the provider terminates for your payment default (a defined material breach under the new Section 4.6), it is unclear whether you owe 100% of remaining Fees or the reduced 50%/6-month amount. The edited Section 4.4(b) references 'if Customer terminates... other than for Lattice's uncured material breach,' but does not address the scenario where Lattice terminates for Customer's breach. This gap could be exploited in a dispute.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: The ambiguity creates a five- or six-figure tail-liability dispute in any scenario where the provider terminates for your breach. If the provider terminates in month 6 of a 36-month term due to your payment default, do you owe 30 months of full Fees (100% tail) or 6 months of half Fees (50% cap)? The accepted edits do not resolve this, and the provider will argue for the higher amount. The language should be tightened to avoid litigation over this threshold issue.
coherence_findings.position: Clarify that the reduced tail liability applies only when you terminate voluntarily, and that provider termination for your material breach results in 100% tail liability for the remaining term.
coherence_findings.fallback: Clarify that the reduced tail applies to any termination by either party other than provider termination for your uncured material breach of payment, confidentiality, or usage restrictions.
coherence_findings.suggested_text: ANNOTATE INTERNAL INCONSISTENCY: The accepted edit to Section 4.4(b) (termination-remedies-analyst-002) replaces 100% tail liability with '50% of Fees... not to exceed six (6) months of Fees' for Customer termination without cause. However, the newly inserted Section 4.6 (termination-remedies-analyst-003) defines 'material breach' to include payment default continuing more than 30 days after notice. Under the edited framework: (1) If you miss payment for 30+ days, that is a defined material breach under Section 4.6(a); (2) Provider terminates under Section 4.3 with 30-day cure; (3) You then owe the REDUCED tail liability (50%, max 6 months) under the edited Section 4.4(b) because the termination is ostensibly 'by Customer' even though it was triggered by Provider notice of breach. The definition of material breach in Section 4.6 makes payment default a termination trigger, but the tail-liability edit in Section 4.4(b) does not distinguish between Customer voluntary termination and Provider termination for Customer breach. This creates ambiguity as to whether breach-termination by Provider triggers the old 100% tail or the new 50%/6-month cap. RECOMMENDATION: Clarify that the reduced tail liability in Section 4.4(b) applies only to voluntary Customer termination for convenience (which does not exist under the Agreement as written), and that termination by Provider for Customer breach results in 100% tail liability.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 9.3: Customer's obligation to pay Fees is suspended during any period in which Lattice is unable to provide the Subscription Services due to a Force Majeure Event affecting Lattice.
coherence_findings.proposed_text: ANNOTATE POTENTIAL CONFLICT WITH SECTION 2.3 SLA SOLE REMEDY: The accepted edit to Section 9.3 (termination-remedies-analyst-006) suspends your Fee obligation when the provider cannot deliver service due to force majeure. However, Section 2.3 states that service credits are your 'sole and exclusive remedies for failures to meet such service-level commitments' and that 'Customer's sole remedy in the event of repeated failures shall be the right to terminate.' If a force majeure event causes a service outage, it is unclear whether: (1) the outage is excluded from SLA calculations (making Section 2.3 inapplicable and the force majeure fee suspension the operative remedy), or (2) the outage counts against SLA and triggers service credits under Section 2.3, in which case the force majeure fee suspension in Section 9.3 contradicts the sole-remedy framing in Section 2.3. The contract does not state that force majeure events are excluded from SLA measurement. RECOMMENDATION: Add express language to Section 2.3 or Section 9.3 stating that force majeure events are excluded from SLA availability calculations, and that the fee-suspension mechanism in Section 9.3 is the sole remedy for force-majeure-caused outages.
coherence_findings.external_comment: The force majeure fee suspension you negotiated may conflict with the SLA sole-remedy provision. If a two-week provider-side force majeure outage triggers both the SLA service-credit mechanism (Section 2.3) and the force majeure fee suspension (edited Section 9.3), the provider may argue that service credits are your sole remedy and fee suspension does not apply. Alternatively, you may argue fee suspension applies but service credits do not. The contract should clarify which regime governs force-majeure-caused outages.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: In a major force majeure event causing extended outage (e.g., AWS region failure, pandemic-related infrastructure collapse), the difference between service credits (typically 10–25% of monthly fee as a credit) and full fee suspension (100% of fee for the outage period) is significant. The ambiguity creates a five-figure dispute in any such event. Clarifying that force majeure outages are excluded from SLA and governed solely by the fee-suspension mechanism eliminates the conflict and ensures you receive the negotiated relief.
coherence_findings.position: Add language to Section 9.3 or Section 2.3 clarifying that force majeure events are excluded from SLA calculations and that the fee-suspension mechanism is the sole remedy for force-majeure-caused service interruptions.
coherence_findings.fallback: Clarify that force majeure fee suspension applies in addition to (not instead of) SLA service credits, and that you receive both remedies if a force majeure event also breaches SLA commitments.
coherence_findings.suggested_text: ANNOTATE POTENTIAL CONFLICT WITH SECTION 2.3 SLA SOLE REMEDY: The accepted edit to Section 9.3 (termination-remedies-analyst-006) suspends your Fee obligation when the provider cannot deliver service due to force majeure. However, Section 2.3 states that service credits are your 'sole and exclusive remedies for failures to meet such service-level commitments' and that 'Customer's sole remedy in the event of repeated failures shall be the right to terminate.' If a force majeure event causes a service outage, it is unclear whether: (1) the outage is excluded from SLA calculations (making Section 2.3 inapplicable and the force majeure fee suspension the operative remedy), or (2) the outage counts against SLA and triggers service credits under Section 2.3, in which case the force majeure fee suspension in Section 9.3 contradicts the sole-remedy framing in Section 2.3. The contract does not state that force majeure events are excluded from SLA measurement. RECOMMENDATION: Add express language to Section 2.3 or Section 9.3 stating that force majeure events are excluded from SLA availability calculations, and that the fee-suspension mechanism in Section 9.3 is the sole remedy for force-majeure-caused outages.
